title: Function Overloading
description: Give multiple versions of a function the same name.

slides.title: Overloading 👀
slides.content:
With **function overloading**, multiple functions can have the completely same name, AS LONG AS their parameters are different!

This is insanely useful.

slides.title: Consider these two functions:
slides.content: We have two math functions. One accepts integers, the other accepts decimals (doubles).

slides.title: The Problem 🤦
slides.content:
It is annoying to have to remember two different names (`addInts` and `addDoubles`) for operations that are essentially the same.

Let's apply function overloading!

slides.title: Overloading at Work
slides.content: We can just name them both 'add'!

quizData.question: How does C++ know which version of the 'add' function to run?

options.text: It runs both of them randomly
It checks the data types of the arguments you passed to the function call
It always runs the first one it finds

quizData.explanation: The compiler looks at the number and type of arguments. Since 5.5 is a double, it seeks out the add() function that accepts doubles.

quizData.question: You can have two completely different functions with the same name, as long as the parameter types or the total number of parameters are different.
quizData.explanation: Yes, this allows the compiler to mathematically distinguish which function you intended to call.
